narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 32, Year 1 grade decile of 6th (slightly above the typical 5.71 average), Year 3 decile of 4th (below the 5.53 average), upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and predicted a 61% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 1 grade decile, ranked by their impact on pushing the prediction one way or the other. Your LSAT score of 32, notably lower than the average of 36.7, was the strongest driver toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model a potential gap in the standardized reasoning skills needed for bar success. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, below the typical 3.21, reinforced this concern by suggesting inconsistent academic performance compared to students who usually pass. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 6 pulled in the opposite direction, toward a passing prediction, as it shows you performed solidly relative to your peers early on—better than average and a bright spot in your record. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher LSATs and GPAs aligning with bar passers, your metrics placed you in a riskier group, even with strengths like your full-time commitment and stable family background. While these top factors outweighed others, it's worth noting that bar passage models like this one weigh entrance exam and GPA results heavily because they correlate strongly with outcomes across diverse applicants, including Asian males like yourself. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—many students overcome similar profiles through targeted improvement. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.7 average, strengthening your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses if possible, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your Year 1 momentum. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at this or other programs. You're clearly driven, and with focused steps, you can rewrite this story.
